title: Object Pronouns
intro: Object pronouns replace a noun that receives the action. Instead of saying 'I see the book', you say 'I see it'. They're essential for natural-sounding Spanish.

sections.title: Direct object pronouns (replace the WHAT / WHO)
Where does the pronoun go?
sections.content:
The object pronoun goes BEFORE the conjugated verb:
• Lo veo. (I see it.) ← 'lo' before 'veo'
• Te quiero. (I love you.) ← 'te' before 'quiero'
• Me llama. (She calls me.) ← 'me' before 'llama'

OR attached to the END of an infinitive:
• Voy a comprarlo. (I'm going to buy it.)
• Quiero verte. (I want to see you.)

items.en: I buy it (fem.) at the market.

sections.label: 💡 Key insight — lo vs la
sections.content:
Choose lo or la based on the gender of what you're replacing:
• El libro → Lo compro. (I buy it. — masculine)
• La camisa → La compro. (I buy it. — feminine)
This is why knowing noun gender matters so much!

questions.prompt: El libro... ___ veo. (I see it — masculine)
La camisa... ___ compro. (I buy it — feminine)

questions.explanation: te = you (direct object), quiero = I want/love → Te quiero = I love you ✓

questions.prompt: Las manzanas... ___ como. (I eat them — feminine plural)
questions.explanation: manzanas is feminine plural → las como ✓

questions.prompt: Where does the object pronoun go?
options: Before the verb (or attached to infinitive)
At the end of the sentence
questions.explanation: Object pronouns go BEFORE the conjugated verb: Me llama / Lo veo. Or attached to infinitives: comprarlo ✓
café is masculine → comprarlo ✓ (attached to infinitive)
